action.hypothesis: `two_stage_residual` has established a new K=128 full frontier point, but the sharp K=64 regression suggests the current prototype may be capacity-limited or too brittle outside its initial setting rather than broadly Pareto-dominant yet. The next informative move is a family-specific stabilization pass at the winning K=128 anchor by increasing expansion factor to 32, testing whether more latent capacity lets the staged residual design convert its structural advantage into another quality gain without changing architecture or optimization.
action.summary: Run a `two_stage_residual` proxy stabilization pass at K=128 with `EXPANSION_FACTOR=32`, keeping the current signum/aux-free recipe fixed and changing only expansion factor.
action.self_review: This follows the winning family rather than abandoning it after one lower-K miss, and it changes exactly one variable on the current best K=128 setup. Risk: the prototype may already be width-saturated and EF=32 may add cost without improving FVU, but this is the cleanest next stabilization read after K=64 regressed sharply.
notes_to_memory: `two_stage_residual` remained a real K=128 winner in full, but its K=64 proxy run regressed badly to 0.2899633552879095, so lower-K transfer is not automatic for this family.
This round begins family-specific stabilization on the winning K=128 anchor by testing whether extra latent capacity improves the staged residual design at fixed optimization settings.
next_hypotheses: If `two_stage_residual` with `EXPANSION_FACTOR=32` improves K=128 proxy FVU, promote it to full and continue stabilization around the stronger width.
If EF=32 is flat or worse, test a family-specific sparsity-allocation parameter next rather than continuing generic width sweeps indefinitely.
If quality improves but runtime regresses materially, follow with a targeted performance pass instead of discarding the architecture.
